coredata.dc:title: Preparation, characterization of green tea carbon quantum dots/curcumin antioxidant and antibacterial nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Fangzhou
Li, Peiyuan
Zhang, Ying
Zhong, Haiyi
Yan, Hongjun
Su, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots with good biocompatibility can be used as nanomaterial carriers to improve the bioavailability of curcumin. In this paper, green tea was used as raw material to provide a green synthesis method for preparing carbon dots nanoparticles, and Curcumin was loaded to prepare carbon quantum dots/Curcumin nanocomposites (CQDs/Cur). A series of characterization methods were used to study the morphology and chemical structure of composite nanoparticles. The tea carbon dots had the characteristics of excitation wavelength dependence, salt solution stability and light stability. Finally, the antioxidant activities of CQDs/Cur nanocomposites were investigated by DPPH, ABTS•+, hydroxyl radical, metal ion chelating and reducing power assays. The compound system not only improved the antioxidant performance of curcumin, but also retained the antibacterial activity of curcumin. These results suggested that tea carbon quantum dots loaded with curcumin had the potential to be developed as natural antioxidants and antibacterial agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Curcumin
Carbon quantum dots
Antioxidant

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis spectroscopy analysis and fluorescence spectrum analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Standard Research of Zhuang and Yao Ethnic Medicine
Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Guangxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51961009 ), the Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Guangxi Province (Gui Ke ZY22096010), GuangXi Distinguished Expert Project (Standard Research of Zhuang and Yao Ethnic Medicine, GUI Talent Tong Zi (2019) No. 13).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of CQDs/Cur nanocomplexes.

(A) TEM image and (B) particle size distribution picture of CQDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs, Cur and CQDs/Cur. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of Cur. (F) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs/Cur. (G) Fluorescence stability of CQDs at different pH values. (H) Fluorescence stability of CQDs solutions with different concentrations of NaCl. (I) Fluorescence stability of CQDs after different time of UV irradiation.
UV absorption spectra and digital images of aqueous solutions of CQDs and CQDs/Cur (Cur solution on the left and CDs/Cur on the right in the same digital photo).
(A) Infrared spectra of CQDs, Cur, CQDs/Cur. (B) XRD spectrum of CQDs, Cur and CQDs/Cur. (C) TGA and (D) DSC thermograms of CQDs, Cur and CQDs/Cur.

(A) XPS survey spectrum of CQDs. (B) XPS survey spectrum of CQDs/Cur. (C)XPS C 1 s peaks and fitting curves of CQDs. (D) XPS O1s peaks and fitting curves of CQDs. (E) XPS N1s peaks and fitting curves of CQDs. (F) XPS C 1 s peaks and fitting curves of CQDs/Cur. (G) XPS O1s peaks and fitting curves of CQDs/Cur. (H) XPS N1s peaks and fitting curves of CQDs/Cur.
DPPH radical scavenging activity of (A) CQDs, (B) CQDs/Cur and (C) Cur. ABTS•+ radical scavenging activity of (D) CQDs, (E) CQDs/Cur and (F) Cur.

Fe2+chelating ability of (A) CQDs, (B) CQDs/Cur and (C) Cur. OH− radical scavenging activity of (D) CQDs, (E) CQDs/Cur and (F) Cur. (G)Survival rate of S. aureus in different drug concentrations. (H) Photographs of antimicrobial activity of CQDs, Cur and CQDs/Cur against S. aureus.
Cell survival rate after treatment with different concentrations of CQDs, Cur and CQDs/Cur.

head.ce:title: Preparation, characterization of green tea carbon quantum dots/curcumin antioxidant and antibacterial nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Guangxi Key Laborary of Zhuang and Yao Ethnic Medicine, Guangxi University of Chinese Medicine, Nanning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmacy, Guangxi Key Laborary of Zhuang and Yao Ethnic Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Pharmacy, Guangxi Key Laborary of Zhuang and Yao Ethnic Medicine, Guangxi University of Chinese Medicine, Nanning, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning ,China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bGuangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots with good biocompatibility can be used as nanomaterial carriers to improve the bioavailability of curcumin. In this paper, green tea was used as raw material to provide a green synthesis method for preparing carbon dots nanoparticles, and Curcumin was loaded to prepare carbon quantum dots/Curcumin nanocomposites (CQDs/Cur). A series of characterization methods were used to study the morphology and chemical structure of composite nanoparticles. The tea carbon dots had the characteristics of excitation wavelength dependence, salt solution stability and light stability. Finally, the antioxidant activities of CQDs/Cur nanocomposites were investigated by DPPH, ABTS•+, hydroxyl radical, metal ion chelating and reducing power assays. The compound system not only improved the antioxidant performance of curcumin, but also retained the antibacterial activity of curcumin. These results suggested that tea carbon quantum dots loaded with curcumin had the potential to be developed as natural antioxidants and antibacterial agents.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin (Cur) has a special diketone structure, which is a kind of orange yellow crystalline powder with bitter taste. In addition to being a natural food additive and colorant, curcumin has been proved to have high biological activities, such as nerve protection, anti-cancer, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant activities. However, the solubility of curcumin in water is only 13 μg/mL, so it is not easy to be absorbed and the bioavailability is poor [17], which restricts the use of curcumin in the food and medical field. In recent years, many materials (micelles, nanoemulsions or microemulsions, liposomes [10], cyclodextrins, solid dispersions [22], etc.) have been developed to improve their bioavailability.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also known as carbon dots, are a new kind of carbon-based nanomaterials, whose surface are rich in amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and other organic functional groups. CQDs have excellent properties such as extremely small size (less than 10 nm (Y. [25])), excellent water solubility, low toxicity, low cost and stable fluorescence performance, which have potential application prospects in many fields such as biological imaging [30], organic analysis and clinical medicine [8, 23, 26]. In recent years, the development of carbon quantum dots carrier materials has attracted extensive attention of scientist. Liang et al. [13] prepared naringin-loaded β-cyclodextrin nanoparticles combined with CQDs. The addition of carbon quantum dots not only enhanced the antioxidant activity of nanoparticles, but also improved the encapsulation efficiency of naringin. Wang et al. [24] developed a EDTA-chitosan-CQDs complexes, in which CQDs can not only provide fluorescence near infrared imaging and photothermal effect, but also acted as a transmembrane carrier to deliver doxorubicin (DOX) drug molecules to tumor cells, achieving highly effective cancer cell inhibition. Based on previous studies, the loading of CQDs carbon nanomaterials is expected to enhance the biological activity of curcumin, but most of these synthesis methods require expensive raw materials and toxic organic reagents.

Green tea is one of the main types of tea in China, which has tea polyphenols, free amino acids, flavonoids, and other active ingredients. Green tea has anti-aging, anti-cancer effect [18, 28], which can adjust blood fat comprehensively [1], reduce cholesterol, and have certain inhibition to the formation of atherosclerosis [11]. It can also eliminate excessive free radicals in human body and protect cell membrane structure, and act as an ideal natural antioxidant. It has been shown that the main antioxidant components in tea are epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), epigallocatechin gallate (ECG), and epicatechin (EC), which are antioxidants by donating hydrogen to free radicals (possibly hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide (O2 •–), or hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)). Among them, many other factors related to the binding affinity of EGCG to some macromolecules (such as proteins and carbohydrates) may contribute to its antioxidant capacity to scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS). [19] It has also been reported that EC appears to regenerate oxidized curcumin, thus acting synergistically antioxidant effect. [21] Xiang et al. [27] prepared a series of functional polyphenol nanoparticles based on renewable green tea extracts (tea polyphenols and theophylline) through covalent copolymerization and non-covalent supramolecular self-assembly pathways, and these polyphenol nanoparticles also exhibited good free radical scavenging properties. The potential of tea in free radical scavenging properties has been clearly demonstrated.

In this paper, considering the merit of green tea and CQDs, green tea was used as raw material to prepare CQDs, which were combined with curcumin to construct Carbon quantum dots/Curcumin nanocomposites (CQDs/Cur), in order to improve the solubility and antioxidant performance of curcumin. Optical properties and structural characteristics of nanomaterials were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, thermogravimetric analysis, and other methods. DPPH free radical scavenging capacity, ABTS free radical scavenging capacity, Fe2+ chelating capacity and hydroxyl free radical scavenging capacity of CQDs/Cur material were studied to explore its antioxidant properties. Evaluation of antimicrobial properties of composite systems was evaluated. This work can explore the application of tea carbon quantum dots in food industry as green antioxidants.

Curcumin (Cur, 98% purity) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., LTD. The green tea was obtained from Guangxi Shunlai Tea Company and was ground into powder for use. Potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), ferrous sulfate (FeSO4), ferric chloride (FeCl3), potassium cyanide [K3Fe(CN)6], sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Kelong Chemical (Chengdu) Co., LTD. All reagents used are of high purity.

The green tea powder (0.5 g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (30 mL), ultrasonic for 10 min using an ultrasonic processor (JP-040S, Shenzhen Jiemeng Cleaning Equipment Co., LTD). The solution was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave chamber and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was poured into the centrifuge tube, centrifuged (5500 r/min) for 10 min, to remove the sediment. The supernatant was filtered with a 0.1 μM filter membrane. The dispersions were freeze-dried using vacuum freeze dryer (LC-10N-50A, Shanghai Lichen Bangxi Instrument Technology Co., LTD) for 24 h to get solid powder samples of green tea carbon quantum dots.

Tea carbon dots and curcumin solution (1 mg/mL) were prepared and mixed according to volume ratio of VCQDs: VCur = 2:1. At room temperature, the mixture was stirred with a magnetic stirrer (2 × 15–3, Shanghai Sile Instrument Co., LTD) for 24 h, lyophilized to give carbon quantum dots/Curcumin nanocomposites (CQDs/Cur).

The morphology of CQDs, Cur, CQDs/Cur was studied by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, HT7700, Japan). A certain concentration of CQDs, Cur, CQDs/Cur was prepared, and the absorption spectrum was determined by UV–VIS spectrophotometer (UV-5200 PC, Shanghai Yuantong Instrument Co., LTD), with scanning range of 200 −800 nm. Fluorescence spectrometer (RF6000, Island ferry) was used to measure emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths to analyze the changes of fluorescence intensity of samples. After mixed grinding with potassium bromide: sample = 90:1, the samples were characterized by infrared spectrometer (iS10, Thermo Nicolet). The sample morphology was observed by transmission electron microscope (HT7700, Hitachi, Ltd).

500 g of tea powder was extracted by distillation in a volatile oil extractor for 6 h. The obtained extract was extracted for three times with an appropriate amount of ethyl acetate, and the extracts were combined, dried, and dehydrated with anhydrous sodium sulfate, then filtered through a 0.2 μM membrane to obtain a yellow concentrated oil. A sample solution of 10 mg/mL CQDs was prepared in 1 mL of ethyl acetate, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min, dried with anhydrous sodium sulfate to remove any trace of water, and then analyzed by GC–MS (Trace 13000s TSQ8000 (Evo DUO)).

The radical scavenging activity was measured using the DPPH method with some modifications [15]. CQDs, CQDs/Cur, Cur solutions with different concentrations (2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64 μg/mL) and DPPH solution (0.175 M) were prepared, respectively. 2 mL of drug solution was mixed with 2 mL of DPPH solution, and the mixture was kept in dark for 10 min. The absorbance of the reaction solution at 517 nm was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The scavenging ability was calculated using the following formula: DPPH radical scavenging rate (%) = (A0-A)/A0 × 100%, where A0 was the absorbance of the control reaction and A was the absorbance of the examined samples.

Different concentrations of CQDs, CQDs/Cur and Cur were prepared (50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 μg·mL−1), respectively. 6.7 mg of solid potassium sulfate was added to 7 mM of ABTS solution to obtain 2.45 mM of preparation solution, which was left at room temperature for 2 days. ABTS preparation solution was diluted with water until its absorbance at 732 nm is 0.7 ± 0.02. 0.1 mL of drug solution was mixed with 2 mL of ABTS solution, reacted in the dark for 10 min to measure its absorbance. The scavenging ability was calculated using the following formula: ABTS radical scavenging rate (%): (A0-A) /A0 × 100%, where A0 was the absorbance of the control reaction and A was the absorbance of the examined samples.

CQDs, CQDs/Cur, Cur was prepared into solutions with different concentrations (1, 10, 100, 250, 500, 1000 μg/mL), respectively. 1% K3[Fe(CN)6] solution, 10% trichloroacetic acid solution and 0.1% ferric chloride solution were prepared, respectively. 0.4 mL of solution was mixed with 2.5 mL of PBS solution and 2.5 mL of Fe2+-K3[Fe(CN)6] solution, and the solution was bathed in water at 50 °C for 20 min. 2.5 mL of trichloroacetic acid was added and mixed, then the reaction solution (2.5 mL) was transferred to the centrifuge tube, and 2.5 mL of water and 0.5 mL of ferric chloride was added. After standing at room temperature for 10 min, the solution was poured into cuvette and the absorbance at 700 nm was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The larger A value means greater reducing power of the sample. The same solution without sample was set as blank group.

The concentrations of CQDs, CQDs/Cur and Cur solution was 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120 μg/mL, respectively. 7.5 mM of 1,10-Phenanthroline monohydrate solution, 7.5 mM of ferrous sulfate solution and 0.1% hydrogen peroxide solution was prepared, respectively. According to Table S1, the solution was mixed for reaction, placed in 37 °C water bath for 60 min, poured into cuvette, and the absorbance of the reaction solution at 536 nm was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The scavenging ability was calculated using the following formula:

Hydroxyl radical scavenging ability (%) = [(A3-A2)/(A1-A2)] × 100%. A1 was the absorption of the number 1 group. A2 was the absorption of the number 2 group. A3 was the absorption of the number 3 group. Each sample was repeated three times.

As representatives, Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were adopted in our tests. The 24 h bacterial cultures were diluted with nutrient solution until the OD value at 600 nm was about 0.5. The sample solutions with different concentrations were mixed with the bacteria solution. The bacterial solution was mixed with normal saline as blank group, and incubated in 37 °C incubator for 30 min. The mixtures were diluted to a certain ratio and plated on LB agar plates, and then cultured in a 37 °C incubator for 24 h. The antibacterial properties of CQDs/Cur and Cur were evaluated by colony counting.

The cytotoxicity of the composites was evaluated by studying the effect of CQDs/Cur on the survival of human normal hepatocytes (L0–2). Cell suspensions (200 μL) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1640 nutrient solution were added to 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 in an incubator. CQDs, Cur, and CQDs/Cur (final concentrations of 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200 μg/mL) were added to the cells and incubated for 24 h. CCK-8 (5%, 200 μL) was added to each well and after 2 h of incubation, the absorbance of the solution at 450 nm was measured using an enzyme marker (Epoch2, Bio Tek).

The shape and size of green tea CQDs were characterized using TEM. It can be seen from Fig. 2A that the obtained green tea CQDs were spherical, with large spacing between particles and almost no particles agglomeration, showing the good dispersity of green tea CQDs. The average particle size of green tea CQDs was 3.07 nm (Fig. 2B), which conformed to the basic appearance characteristics of CQDs [29]. Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) analysis (Figure S1, Table S2 and S3) showed that the volatile oil of green tea mainly contained alcohols, alkenes, ketones, alkanes, esters, and other substances. The tea components included antioxidant and antibacterial and anti-inflammatory components, including phytol, catechol, tetradecanoic acid and hexadecanoic acid. It also included polycyclic aromatic compounds such as naphthalene and phenanthrene. Usually, charcoal roasting affects the content of tea leaves, and polycyclic aromatic compounds increase with the increase of charcoal roasting time. Although the preparation process of tea carbon dots involves hydrothermal charring, no increase was observed for polycyclic aromatic compounds, while the amount of catechol, 2,4-di‑tert-butylphenol, phenol, 2,2′-methylenebis increased, which were important intermediates of many antioxidants, indicating that tea carbon dots have more advantages as antioxidants (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis spectroscopy analysis and fluorescence spectrum analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of CQDs and CQDs/Cur were determined by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The UV-visible absorption spectra of synthesized CQDs (Fig. 2 C) showed absorption peaks at 215 nm and 270 nm, which were related to π -π * and n -π * transitions of C = O and C = C bonds, respectively. The characteristic absorption peak of Cur appeared at 424 nm in UV–vis absorption spectrum. When CQDs was mixed with Cur, the characteristic absorption peak of Cur disappeared, and the weak shoulder peak of CQDs/Cur appeared in the range of 450–500 nm, indicating that Cur was introduced on the surface of CQDs.

The optical properties of CQDs and CQDs/Cur were measured by fluorescence spectroscopy. As can be seen from Fig. 2D, when the excitation wavelength of green tea CQDs was 320–420 nm (20 nm apart), fluorescence intensity decreased gradually as it moves to higher wavelengths. The strongest emission was observed at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The best excitation wavelength of green tea CQDs was 320 nm with emission wavelength of 435 nm, and the fluorescence emission of CDQs acted in an excitation-dependent manner. The observed multicolor emission fluorescence may be due to different emission sites and particle sizes on carbon quantum dots [20].

In Fig. 2E, Cur has excitation independence in the range of excitation wavelength 340 to 420 nm. When the excitation wavelength of Cur was 420 nm, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum at emission wavelength of 565 nm. As shown in Fig. 2F, within the range of 500 nm to 650 nm, CQDs/Cur fluorescence peak appeared, which can be attributed to the evolution of Cur fluorescence peak. Compared with free Cur, stokes shift [31] occurred in the fluorescence peak in the range of 500 - 650 nm.

The photoluminescence properties of CQDs were measured by dispersing them into different pH solutions. The results in Fig. 2G showed that the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs was the strongest at pH = 8. With the increase of acidity and alkalinity, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs decreased, indicating that the green tea CQDs was pH-dependent and its fluorescence intensity could be regulated by changing the pH value.

The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence stability of green tea CQDs was studied by detecting the fluorescence intensity of green tea CQDs in different concentrations of NaCl solution. The fluorescence intensity was stable at Na+ concentration of 0–1.0 mol/L (Fig. 2H). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of green tea CQDs was not significantly affected by continuous irradiation of 365 nm UV light even at an irradiation time of 120 min (Fig. 2I). These results indicated that the prepared green tea CQDs had good resistance to ionic strength and photobleaching.

CQDs and CQDs/Cur solution with different concentrations was prepared to study their solubility. Within the studied concentration range (0.01∼1 mg/mL), the aqueous dispersion of curcumin was not homogeneous and was in a mixed state, and the insoluble drug particles were clearly visible at the bottom after placement. As shown in Fig. 3 , the characteristic peaks of pure curcumin samples at different concentration could not be detected, indicating that the curcumin samples were difficult to dissolve and had poor water solubility. However, the CQDs/Cur solution appeared as a homogeneous state with transparent reddish-brown color. The characteristic peak of curcumin appeared at 425 nm was observed for the CQDs/Cur solution, and the peak intensity gradual increased when the CQDs/Cur concentration increased, indicating the enhanced water solubility of CQDs/Cur compared with Cur.

Infrared spectroscopy can reflect the vibration absorption frequency of different chemical bonds and functional groups to determine the structure of compounds, so as to understand the existence state of CQDs, Cur, CQDs/Cur functional groups [14]. As shown in Fig. 4
A, in the CQDs spectrum, the stretching vibration of CH bond caused a small band of 3000 cm−1, and the wide band near 1050 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of CO. It can be seen that in the spectrum of natural curcumin, no peak was found in the carbonyl region (1800–1650 cm−1), which meant that curcumin existed in the form of ketoenol tautomerism. The peak of 1600 cm−1 was considered as C = O stretching vibration, which appeared in the IR spectrum of Curcumin [9]. There was a characteristic peak near 3500 cm−1, which came from the characteristic absorption peak of -OH. The infrared spectrum of CQDs/Cur exhibited the superposition of CQDs spectrum and Cur spectrum. By comparing the infrared spectra of Cur and CQDs/Cur, it is found that the latter has a higher intensity and wider range, indicating that the combination of CQDs and curcumin produced a strong hydrogen bond. When CQDs was coated with curcumin, the characteristic peak at 1280 cm−1 became weak, which may be due to the reason that hydrogen bonding changed the structure of functional groups. The results showed that curcumin had been loaded on CQDs as carriers.

In order to understand the crystal structure changes of curcumin and complex, X-ray diffraction method was used to study the pure Curcumin, CQDs and complex samples. Curcumin showed specific crystal diffraction peaks at 2θ = 8.84°, 14.52°, 17.28° and 18.14°, which could be inferred to conform to high crystal structure. The carbon dots were amorphous powder with only a wide diffraction peak appears at 23° When the carbon dots were loaded with curcumin, the spectra showed not a simple superposition of the two compounds. Angular shift in XRD usually refers to the appearance of doping of certain elements, which makes the cell parameters larger or smaller and the crystal plane spacing larger. CQDs/Cur showed crystal diffraction peaks at 2θ = 24.64°, 49.46° and the peak intensity disappeared or weakened after the loading of curcumin. The possible reason is that curcumin has been encased in carbon dots and its crystal form has significantly changed. The amorphous structure contributes to the dispersibility and bioavailability of curcumin.

The thermal properties of the composite system were further analyzed by thermogravimetry. As shown in the weight loss (TG) plots of Cur and CQDs/Cur, curcumin had only one major weight-loss stage between 25 °C (initial temperature) to 800 °C, and rapidly decompositions from 290 °C to 420 °C, with a mass loss of about 60%. This stage was mainly related to water evaporation and curcumin molecular fracture, which released a large amount of CO2. The remaining phenolic hydroxyl skeleton was continually decomposed at a slow rate at 400–800 °C. The composite system lost 11% of its total mass before 200 °C due to the loss of adsorbed water and water of crystallization. The curve rapidly dropped at 200–350 °C, the sample weight decreasing to 58% of its original weight at 350 °C, and then slowly decomposed to the final temperature of the experiment. The maximum concentration loss of CQDs/Cur complex (289 °C) was lower than that of pure curcumin, which may be due to the loose structure caused by binding effect. The results showed that the structure of curcumin became looser and easier to be pyrolyzed.

The elemental composition and chemical bond configurations of CQDs and CQDs/Cur were determined (Fig. 5). The wide-sweep XPS spectra of CQDs explained the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen at 285.26, 400.15 and 532.07 eV, respectively, indicating that the synthesized CQDs was mainly composed of three elements: C (71.04%), O (20.15%) and N (8.81%). High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of CQDs showed three peaks at 284.68, 286.03, and 288.03 eV, attributed to CC/C = C, CN and C = O, respectively, confirming that the carbon dots were rich in N and O functional groups. On the other hand, slight displacement occurred in CQDs/Cur nanocomposites, and the binding energy of C1s 288.21 eV may be attributed to C = O/C = N. In addition, 531.53 and 532.88 eV of the O1s spectrum of CQDs and 531.44 eV and 532.86 eV of the composite system appeared two peaks with the same characteristics as COH and OC = O/CO-C. The ratio of OC = O/CO-C in CQDs/Cur nanocomposites was higher than that in original CQDs. It conformed to the unsaturated lipid and aromatic groups of curcumin as a diketone compound, indicating the presence of Cur. For CQDs N1s, two peaks appeared at 399.85 eV and 401.45 eV, which were attributed to CN-C and NO. For N1s of composite system, the peaks also appeared at similar positions. These observations were consistent with FTIR and TEM data, demonstrating that Cur was successfully loaded into the carbon quantum dots.

The free radical scavenging ability of the sample was evaluated by recording the reduced absorbance of DPPH at 517 nm [3, 12] (from purple to yellow). As shown in Fig. 6 A-C, with the increase of concentration, the scavenging ability of CQDs, CQDs/Cur and Cur on DPPH free radicals increased. In the range of 0–32 μg/mL, the scavenging activity of CQDs and Cur increased from 4.1% to 88.1%, respectively, from 16.6% to 86.8%. Curcumin's ability to scavenge DPPH radical was mainly due to its two phenolic hydroxyl groups with antioxidant activity. Polynomial fitting regression was performed to calculate the EC50 values of DPPH free radical removal for CQDs, Cur and CQDs/Cur solutions. When the DPPH radical clearance rate reached 50%, the concentration of Cur was 7.2 μg/mL, while the required concentration of CQDs/Cur was 5.4 μg/mL. The antioxidant ability to DPPH free radical follows: CQDs <Cur < CQDs/Cur. The results showed that the prepared green tea CQDs had good scavenging ability of DPPH free radical. Compared with Cur of the same content, the DPPH free radical scavenging ability of Cur was further improved by combining CQDs with Cur.

The mechanism for eliminating ABTS•+ radicals is electron transfer reaction. Considering that ABTS•+ radicals have a positive charge, in order to form a stable neutral molecule, it is necessary to obtain an electron from the molecule of the antioxidant [4, 6]. As shown in Fig. 6D-F, with the increase of CQDs concentration, the scavenging ability of Cur and CQDs/Cur on ABTS radical increased. In the experimental concentration range, the clearance rate of CQDs/Cur increased from 21.3% to 79.3%. Performed polynomial fitting regression calculation, when ABTS radical scavenging rate reached 50%, the concentrations of CQDs, Cur and CQDs/Cur were 89.1, 81.9 and 48.6 μg/mL, respectively. The results showed that the combination of curcumin and green tea CQDs could further improved the free radical scavenging ability of curcumin.

Transition metals play a catalytic role in lipid oxidation. As an important oxidation promoter, Fe2+ has high reactivity in transition metals [2]. Therefore, using antioxidants in the ferrous ion chelation process, the result is an indicator of antioxidant capacity, in which the higher absorbance of the sample indicates the stronger antioxidant capacity [5]. As shown in Fig. 7 A-C, the absorbance at 700 nm (A700) increased from 0.03 to 0.44 for Cur, indicating that Cur had potential antioxidant capacity. With the increase of initial concentration (from 0.33 μg/mL to 333.33 μg/mL), the A700 value of CQDs/Cur increased from 0.03 to 0.97. Under the same Cur content, the CQDs/Cur nanocomposites had stronger chelating ability to ferrous ions.

Hydroxyl radical, as a member of oxygen-group free radicals with strong toxicity, can react with DNA, amino acids, lipids and proteins in organisms, causing irreversible oxidative stress, which has a great impact on biological health. Similarly, by breaking peptide bonds, hydroxyl radicals produce carbonyl groups, which disrupt the primary structure of proteins and cause damage to human cells. As shown in Fig. 7D-F, the CQDs/Cur nanocomposites had the scavenging activity of hydroxyl radical [7], which enhanced with the increase of drug concentration. When the concentration of CQDs/Cur was 40 μg/mL, the OH-clearance rate reached 77.49%, while higher concentration of curcumin was needed to achieve same level of clearance rate. The IC50 value of CQDs, Cur and CQDs/Cur was 41.6, 111.8 and 19.4 μg/mL, respectively. CQDs/Cur showed significantly higher antioxidant activity than Cur.

At present, the international widely used food antioxidants contain Butyl hydroxy anisole (BHA), Butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT), gallic acid, citric acid, ascorbic acid, and other acid synergists, etc. They can be used alone or in combination to meet the needs of most food products. In a similar DPPH scavenging assay, the EC50 value of the commercial standard BHT was 39.1 ± 0.3 μg/mL (Ferraz et al., 2021), which is approximately seven times higher than that of CQDs/Cur (5.4 μg/mL). In the study by Rabia Naz et al. [16] the EC50 value of the standards gallic acid and ascorbic acid was 28 ± 1.1 and 39 ± 1.6 μg/mL, respectively, which showed higher hydroxyl radical scavenging compared to CQDs/Cur (IC50 value of 19.4 μg/mL). However, the ABTS radical scavenging activity of CQDs/Cur was lower than that of ascorbic acid (IC50 value of 7.5 ± 0.1 µg/mL) and BHA (IC50 value of 4.22 ± 0.07 µg/mL). In conclusion, CQDs/Cur has a great potential to be used as a powerful antioxidant in the food industry.

To study the antibacterial performance of the samples, S. aureus suspension was incubated with CQDs/Cur and Cur at different concentrations, respectively. The pure CQDs showed weak antibacterial effect (Figure S1), and when the concentration of CQDs was as high as 250 μg/mL, S. aureus still had 73.8% survival rate. As shown in the Fig. 7H, it can be intuitively seen that with the increase of curcumin concentration, the number of bacterial colonies on the surface of the petri dish gradually decreased. According to the statistics of the plate count, with the increase of concentration from 0.27 μM to 0.54 μM, the survival rate of S. aureus under pure curcumin decreased from 43.9% to 0.3%. However, CQDs/Cur control group with the same curcumin content showed slightly weakened but still significant antibacterial effect against S. aureus, and the sterilization rate was over 99.9%. The results showed that the antibacterial effect of curcumin was not affected by the loading of carbon dots.

The CCK-8 colorimetric method was used to test the biotoxicity of carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 8 , human normal hepatocytes were incubated in samples with different mass concentrations (12.5, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200 μg/mL) for 24 h. It was found that the cellular activity was 83.4% even at a mass concentration of 200 μg/mL of CQDs. This indicates that the carbon dots have a rather low cytotoxicity and can even promote cell proliferation. Curcumin has a high cytotoxicity, but the cytotoxicity was significantly lower after loading on CQDs. Cell viability remained high (≥70%) within the concentration required for CQDs/Cur as an antioxidant material, indicating that the nanocomposite had no substantial effect on cellular activity.

In this study, carbon quantum dots were prepared by hydrothermal method using green tea as raw material and loaded with curcumin to construct a composite nanomaterial system using a green, economic and environment-friendly method. The properties of green tea carbon quantum dots are characterized including excitation wavelength dependence, salt solution stability and certain anti-photobleaching properties, pH-adjustability. The prepared CQDs/Cur had good dispersibility and improved solubility in water. The antioxidant activity of CQDs/Cur was stronger than that of free Cur and CQDs, and antioxidant performance enhanced with the increase of concentration. The loading of tea carbon retained the excellent antibacterial performance of curcumin. Green tea carbon quantum dots have the potential to be developed into natural antioxidant, which lays a foundation for screening functional foods and drugs with strong antioxidant capacity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51961009), the Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Guangxi Province (Gui Ke ZY22096010), GuangXi Distinguished Expert Project (Standard Research of Zhuang and Yao Ethnic Medicine, GUI Talent Tong Zi (2019) No. 13).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2022.134247.
